These deaths are accepted as God's will until the arrival of a new police inspector, Vishwas Saranjame, who openly challenges the belief and declares that what the village calls faith is actually a pattern of murder hidden behind fear and ritual. Soon after, a man named Rajan Nimkar is found dead in the village pond, and the villagers immediately announce that Shankasur has punished another sinner. Vishwas orders a post mortem and begins a detailed investigation, during which he finds a gold chain near the pond that leads him to Shankar Mahadik, a young man who had publicly fought with Rajan and owed him money. As the investigation progresses, the evidence begins to blur when DNA reports rule out Shankar, the white hair is identified as wild cow hair, and the whip marks are traced to nylon cloth, all materials used in the Shankasur costume. Vishwas turns his suspicion toward the three Shankasur performers of Devtali, Ganesh Korde, Sadanand Rangne, and Raghunath Pawase, but with no direct proof and rising political pressure, he is forced to release them, strengthening the villagers' belief that Shankasur is real and beyond the law. His search leads him to the village tailor Shafiq, who reveals that such wool is used in the Shankasur costume of another village called Palner. Forensic tests finally confirm that the wool fibers and whip marks match Raghunath's costume, while the skin fragments are traced to a separate domestic incident, clearing other suspects. Vishwas arrests Raghunath and sends him for psychiatric treatment, finally ending a cycle of ritualistic killings and forcing Devtali to confront the horrifying truth that their god was only a mask worn by a fractured mind.
